q1_type_of_injury.text: What type of injury is this?

q2_fall_height.text: How far did the patient fall? Approximately how many metres or storeys?
q2_fall_height.guidance_note: Falls from more than 1 metre or more than 1 storey in adults, or any fall in children or elderly patients with head-first landing, should be treated as high-mechanism trauma with potential spinal injury.

q4_fall_landing.text: How did the patient land â€” on their feet, back, side, or head?

q5_fall_head_first.text: A fall from this height landing head-first is very serious. Is the patient unconscious, moving their limbs, or complaining of neck or back pain?

q3_penetrating_severity.text: Is this a stab wound, gunshot, or other penetrating injury? And where is the wound located â€” chest, abdomen, limb, or elsewhere?

q7_limb_bleeding.text: Is there active bleeding from the wound? Is it spurting, heavy, or soaking through dressings?

q6_burn_extent.text: How extensive is the burn? Estimate the area affected â€” for example, is it smaller than the palm of the patient's hand, about the size of a dinner plate, or covering a large area of the body?

q7_burn_location.text: Where is the burn located? Is it on the face, hands, feet, genitals, or over a joint?

q8_industrial_type.text: What type of workplace incident is this?

q9_general_conscious.text: Is the patient conscious and able to respond?

q10_general_bleeding.guidance_note: Direct pressure is the first-line measure for all external bleeding. Do not remove dressings to check â€” add more material on top if blood soaks through.

pre_arrival_instructions: Stay on the line with me.
Do not move the patient's neck or back.
Keep the patient as still as possible.
If you are trained, stabilise the head in the position found.
If there is immediate danger â€” fire, traffic â€” carefully slide the patient to safety without twisting their neck.
Stay on the line with me.
Do not move the patient's neck or back unless there is immediate danger.
Keep the patient still and reassure them help is on the way.
If the patient is unconscious, check their breathing.
If they are not breathing, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
Do not move the patient unless there is immediate danger.
Keep the patient still and comfortable.
If anything changes â€” pain, difficulty breathing, loss of consciousness â€” tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
This is a penetrating injury to the chest or abdomen â€” help is on the way.
Do not remove any objects from the wound.
If there is bleeding, apply firm direct pressure around the object.
If the patient is conscious, help them lie on their back.
If there is a wound in the chest and you hear a sucking sound when they breathe, cover the wound with your hand on three sides, leaving one side open.
Stay on the line with me.
Apply firm, continuous direct pressure to the wound.
Do not lift the pressure to check the wound.
If blood soaks through, add more material on top.
Elevate the limb above the level of the heart if possible while maintaining pressure.
Stay on the line with me.
Do not remove the object if it is still embedded in the wound.
Apply a clean dressing around the wound.
Keep the limb still and elevated if possible.
Watch for bleeding and tell me if it starts.
Stay on the line with me.
Cool the burn with cool (not cold) running water for at least 10 minutes if possible.
Remove clothing and jewellery near the burn before swelling starts, unless it is stuck to the skin.
Cover the burn loosely with a clean plastic wrap or cling film â€” this protects the wound and reduces pain.
Do not apply creams, oils, or toothpaste to the burn.
Keep the patient warm â€” cover uninjured areas with a blanket.
Stay on the line with me.
This is a serious burn â€” help is on the way.
Remove the patient from the source of the burn if it is safe to do so.
Cool the burn with cool running water for at least 10 minutes if possible.
Do not remove clothing that is stuck to the skin.
Cover the burn loosely with cling film or a clean sheet.
Keep the patient warm â€” cover uninjured areas.
If the patient becomes unconscious, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
A burn to the face, hands, feet, or genitals requires specialist care â€” help is on the way.
Cool the burn with cool running water for at least 10 minutes if possible.
For face burns: do not apply ice. Keep the face exposed and cool.
Do not remove clothing stuck to the skin.
Cover the burn loosely with cling film.
Stay on the line with me.
A crush injury is serious â€” help is on the way.
Do NOT try to lift the object off the patient until the ambulance crew arrives, unless there is immediate life-threatening danger.
Keep the patient as calm and still as possible.
If the patient is conscious, reassure them.
Cover any visible wounds with a clean dressing.
Stay on the line with me.
Do NOT touch the patient while they are in contact with the electrical source.
Switch off the power at the source if you can do so safely.
Once the power is off and it is safe, check if the patient is breathing.
If not breathing, start CPR.
Electrical burns may be small on the skin but very serious inside â€” do not underestimate this injury.
Stay on the line with me.
Remove contaminated clothing if it can be removed easily â€” do not pull clothing over the face.
Flush the affected area with large amounts of clean water for at least 15-20 minutes.
Do not try to neutralise the chemical.
If the chemical was splashed in the eyes, flush the eyes with clean water for 15-20 minutes.
Do not apply any creams or ointments.
Stay on the line with me.
The patient is unconscious following trauma â€” help is on the way.
Check if the patient is breathing.
If not breathing, open the airway and check again.
If still not breathing, start CPR.
Do not move the patient's neck or back unless there is immediate danger.
Stay on the line with me.
Apply firm, continuous direct pressure to any bleeding wounds.
Do not lift pressure to check the wound.
If blood soaks through, add more material on top.
If the patient is conscious, keep them calm and still.
Stay on the line with me.
An ambulance is on the way.
Keep the patient still and comfortable.
Apply a clean dressing to any wounds.
If anything changes â€” increased pain, new bleeding, difficulty breathing, or loss of consciousness â€” tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
Cool the burn with cool (not cold) running water for at least 10 minutes if possible.
Cover the burn loosely with a clean plastic wrap or cling film.
Do not apply creams, oils, or toothpaste to the burn.
Keep the patient warm â€” cover uninjured areas with a blanket.
A smaller burn away from joints, face, or hands can be managed with first aid â€” but if you are unsure, stay on the line.
